ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general ice cream food product class; no dedicated soft-serve subclass exists. Closest defensible match used.
ontology.foodex2_description: Ice cream and similar frozen desserts
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC covers ice cream and similar frozen desserts; no soft-serve-specific code exists. Closest defensible match used.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard soft serve cone weighing approximately 120 g, as commonly served at fast-food outlets and dessert stalls in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Soft Serve Ice Cream — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input record carries HPB provenance at confidence 0.95 for all per_100g fields. Values preserved as supplied. Saturated fat cross-referenced against USDA FoodData Central NDB 19270 (Frozen desserts, ice cream, soft serve, chocolate) and USDA SR Legacy 19095 (Ice creams, vanilla, soft-serve) for plausibility; HPB values accepted as primary.

gi_source.title: International table of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2002 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI value of 57 (medium) for vanilla ice cream is drawn from the University of Sydney GI Database and the Atkinson et al. 2008 international GI table (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283), which lists regular vanilla ice cream at GI ~57. Soft serve is compositionally similar to regular vanilla ice cream (milk, cream, sugar, vanilla) and is accepted as a defensible analogous match. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (57 × 27.96) / 100 ≈ 15.9, using 120 g serving × 23.3 g carbohydrate per 100 g = 27.96 g carbohydrate per serving.

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar content (19.6 g/100 g) promoting insulin-mediated sodium retention; sodium at 58 mg/100 g is moderate but cumulative intake from a full serving is relevant.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit soft serve ice cream to occasional very small portions, as the high sugar load (19.6 g/100 g) drives insulin-mediated sodium retention and may elevate blood pressure; choose lower-sugar frozen alternatives such as plain frozen yogurt when possible.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar at 19.6 g/100 g exceeds the 15 g/100 g diabetes threshold; combined with 23.3 g/100 g refined carbohydrate and a medium GI of 57, this food causes rapid postprandial glucose elevation.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with type 2 diabetes should avoid or strictly limit soft serve ice cream because its high sugar content (19.6 g/100 g) and medium GI (57) produce a high glycemic load per serving (~15.9), significantly elevating postprandial blood glucose; if consumed, restrict to a very small portion and monitor blood glucose closely.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat (~2.4 g/100 g) and high added sugar (19.6 g/100 g) contribute to dyslipidaemia and endothelial inflammation relevant to cardiovascular risk.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume soft serve ice cream only occasionally and in small portions, as the combination of saturated fat (~2.4 g/100 g) and high sugar (19.6 g/100 g) may adversely affect LDL cholesterol and triglyceride levels, increasing cardiovascular risk per AHA dietary guidelines.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Soft serve ice cream is a frozen dairy dessert dispensed through a machine that aerates the mixture as it freezes, producing a lighter, creamier texture than scooped hard ice cream. The vanilla variety is made from a base of milk, cream, sugar, vanilla flavouring, stabilisers, and emulsifiers, and is typically served in a wafer cone or cup. Per 100 g, it provides 143 kcal, 3.0 g protein, 3.88 g fat (approximately 2.4 g saturated), 23.3 g carbohydrate, and 19.6 g sugar, with 58 mg sodium and a notable 149 mg calcium from the dairy base. The dietary fibre value of 2.91 g/100 g is atypically high for soft serve and likely reflects added stabilisers or cone contribution rather than whole-food fibre. Soft serve has a medium glycemic index (~57) and a high glycemic load per standard 120 g serving (~15.9), making it a high-concern food for individuals managing type 2 diabetes or metabolic syndrome. The high sugar content also raises concerns for hypertension (insulin-mediated sodium retention) and cardiovascular disease (dyslipidaemia). Soft serve is widely available at fast-food chains, hawker centres, and dessert stalls across Singapore and Southeast Asia, and is a popular snack for all age groups. It is not suitable for vegans or those with dairy or gluten (cone) allergies.

confidence_reason: All core per_100g nutrient values are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database at confidence 0.95. GI value is drawn from the University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2008 peer-reviewed journal using a defensible analogous match (vanilla ice cream). Saturated fat is estimated from USDA SR Legacy 19095 as HPB does not report it separately; this is the only estimated field.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (2.40 g/100 g) is estimated from USDA SR Legacy entry 19095 (Ice creams, vanilla, soft-serve) as the HPB source does not separately report saturated fat; this value is plausible and consistent with the total fat of 3.88 g/100 g. 2. Dietary fibre at 2.91 g/100 g is atypically high for soft serve ice cream and likely reflects added stabilisers (e.g., carrageenan, guar gum) or cone contribution in the HPB composite entry rather than intrinsic whole-food fibre; this value is preserved from the HPB source but flagged for review. 3. Fields set to -1 (monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g): these micronutrient and fatty acid fractions are not reported in the HPB Foodo entry for this food item and are not available in the Singapore HPB database for this specific record. USDA SR Legacy 19095 reports some of these values but the HPB record is the primary source; detailed fatty acid fractions and full micronutrient panel are not available from HPB for this entry as of 2026. 4. GI of 57 is an analogous estimate based on vanilla ice cream from the University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2008; no direct soft-serve-specific GI measurement was identified in the approved GI sources. 5. Halal status is assumed based on typical commercial soft serve formulations in Singapore; kosher status is not confirmed. 6. The input record listed the condition key as 'diabetes' — this has been updated to 'type2_diabetes' with human_condition_name 'Type 2 Diabetes' per Veritas naming rules. 7. Cardiovascular disease health context has been added as a mandatory condition not present in the original input.
